scenarios.title: Renal / Ureteric Calculi (Urolithiasis)
references: ACR AC Acute Onset Flank Pain

recommendations.modality: NCCT KUB (Non-Contrast CT Kidneys Ureters Bladder)
recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for ureteric and renal calculi. >95% sensitivity and specificity. Detects all stone types including radiolucent uric acid stones. Shows stone size, site, density (HU), and secondary signs of obstruction.
recommendations.india_context: India is in the 'stone belt' — high prevalence, especially in northern and western states. NCCT KUB is the definitive investigation. Low-dose NCCT protocol should be used to reduce radiation. PMJAY covers this investigation.
recommendations.special_notes: Low-dose NCCT (≤3 mSv) is as accurate as standard dose. Stone density (HU) guides ESWL suitability: <1000 HU = likely ESWL-responsive. Measure stone-skin distance for ESWL planning.

recommendations.rationale: First-line in pregnant patients, children, and for monitoring known calculi. Detects hydronephrosis and renal calculi but has lower sensitivity for ureteric stones (especially mid-ureter).
recommendations.india_context: Preferred first-line in pregnancy (mandatory — no radiation). Available everywhere. Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) being adopted in Indian emergency departments.
recommendations.special_notes: Sensitivity for ureteric calculi only 45–60% (poor for mid-ureter). Useful for hydronephrosis detection and follow-up. Ureteric jets on Doppler can confirm ureteric patency.

recommendations.rationale: Preferred alternative to NCCT KUB in pregnancy. Good for hydronephrosis but stones appear as signal voids — less sensitive for small stones.
recommendations.india_context: Use in pregnant women when ultrasound is inconclusive. Available at tertiary centres.
recommendations.special_notes: Heavily T2-weighted sequences (HASTE, FIESTA) used. Gadolinium not usually required. Not as sensitive as NCCT for stone detection.

recommendations.rationale: Identifies radio-opaque stones (calcium oxalate, calcium phosphate, struvite). Misses radiolucent stones (uric acid ~15%) and most ureteric stones. Useful for post-treatment follow-up.
recommendations.india_context: Available everywhere and cheap. Useful to confirm stone passage post-treatment or for serial follow-up.
recommendations.special_notes: 70–80% of renal calculi are radio-opaque. Does not show uric acid or cystine stones well.

recommendations.rationale: Largely replaced by NCCT KUB and CT urography. Requires contrast, provides functional information but inferior anatomical detail for stones.
recommendations.india_context: NCCT KUB is superior in all aspects. IVP should be abandoned in favour of NCCT KUB where available.
recommendations.special_notes: Reserve only if CT is unavailable and anatomical/functional information of collecting system is specifically needed.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for haematuria workup. Three phases (non-contrast, corticomedullary, excretory) evaluate the entire urothelium from renal parenchyma to bladder. Detects masses, calculi, and urothelial tumours.
recommendations.india_context: High prevalence of bladder cancer (second most common urological malignancy in India) and renal cell carcinoma. CTU is first-line for haematuria workup at tertiary centres.
recommendations.special_notes: Whole urinary tract evaluated in one examination. Split-bolus technique reduces radiation dose while maintaining diagnostic quality. Bladder evaluation may still need cystoscopy for small mucosal lesions.

recommendations.rationale: First-line at primary/secondary level. Identifies renal masses, calculi, and bladder masses. Limited sensitivity for small urothelial tumours and upper tract lesions.
recommendations.india_context: Available everywhere and no radiation. Use as initial investigation. If US inconclusive or high-risk features, proceed to CTU.
recommendations.special_notes: Full bladder required for adequate bladder assessment. Add colour Doppler for vascularity of renal masses.

recommendations.rationale: Alternative to CTU when radiation must be minimised (young women, recurrent imaging, renal insufficiency with gadolinium limitations). Good soft tissue detail.
recommendations.india_context: Use in patients with renal insufficiency or contrast allergy where non-contrast CTU is insufficient. Available at tertiary centres.
recommendations.special_notes: Gadolinium may be withheld if eGFR <30 (risk of NSF). Non-contrast MRI still useful for collecting system.

scenarios.title: Renal Mass / Suspected Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC)

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for characterisation and staging of renal masses. Four phases (non-contrast, corticomedullary, nephrographic, excretory) assess enhancement pattern, vascularity, collecting system involvement, and staging.
recommendations.india_context: Incidental renal masses increasingly detected on ultrasound. CT characterises malignant potential and guides nephron-sparing surgery decisions.
recommendations.special_notes: Enhancement >15–20 HU between non-contrast and post-contrast = enhancing mass (malignant until proven otherwise). Bosniak classification for cystic renal masses (I–IV).
contraindicated_if: severe renal failure (contrast — consider non-contrast + MRI)

recommendations.rationale: Superior to CT for indeterminate lesions (Bosniak IIF, III cysts), fat-poor angiomyolipoma, and assessment in renal insufficiency. DWI and chemical shift imaging help characterise subtypes.
recommendations.india_context: Use when CT is inconclusive or renal function is impaired. Available at tertiary centres. Also preferred for young patients to avoid radiation.
recommendations.special_notes: Chemical shift (in-phase vs out-of-phase) detects microscopic fat — clear cell vs other subtypes. DWI: restricted diffusion suggests malignancy.
contraindicated_if: severe renal failure (gadolinium — use non-contrast MRI)

recommendations.rationale: Incidental detection of renal masses. Characterises simple cysts (no further imaging needed) but limited for solid mass characterisation — must proceed to CT or MRI.
recommendations.india_context: Most renal masses in India are incidentally detected on ultrasound. Any solid or complex cystic renal mass on US needs CT/MRI.
recommendations.special_notes: Simple cysts (Bosniak I) on US: anechoic, posterior acoustic enhancement, no septa = no further imaging. Any deviation requires CT.

recommendations.rationale: Lung is the most common site of RCC metastasis. CT chest for pulmonary staging; Tc-99m bone scan if bone pain or ALP elevated.
recommendations.india_context: Stage at diagnosis guides surgical decision. Advanced disease: systemic therapy (TKIs).
recommendations.special_notes: PET-CT not routinely recommended for RCC staging as FDG uptake is variable.

scenarios.title: Recurrent UTI / Suspected Urinary Tract Anomaly

recommendations.rationale: First-line for all children and adults with recurrent UTI. Identifies hydronephrosis, duplex systems, bladder wall thickening, post-void residual, and structural anomalies.
recommendations.india_context: Recurrent UTI in children mandates imaging in India. Antenatal hydronephrosis is common — postnatal follow-up with ultrasound mandatory. Available at all district hospitals.
recommendations.special_notes: Always include post-void residual bladder scan. Check for renal scarring (cortical thinning). Measure both kidneys and ureters.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) grading and urethral anatomy (posterior urethral valves in boys). Essential in children with confirmed UTI and abnormal upper tract imaging.
recommendations.india_context: Recommended in Indian paediatric urology guidelines for children with febrile UTI and hydronephrosis. Posterior urethral valves are a common cause of recurrent UTI in boys.
recommendations.special_notes: Perform when UTI has settled. Grade VUR I–V on MCUG. Grades IV–V require surgery; I–III managed medically with prophylactic antibiotics.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for detecting renal cortical scarring from pyelonephritis/reflux nephropathy. Assesses differential renal function. Essential for long-term management decisions in recurrent UTI with VUR.
recommendations.india_context: Available at nuclear medicine departments in major cities. Used for scarring assessment and differential function before surgical intervention.
recommendations.special_notes: Perform ≥6 months after acute pyelonephritis to distinguish scars from acute lesions. DTPA/MAG-3 for drainage assessment (obstruction).

recommendations.rationale: Comprehensive evaluation of urinary tract anatomy and function without radiation. Ideal for children when multiple investigations needed or complex anomalies (duplex systems, ectopic ureters).
recommendations.india_context: Increasingly used in paediatric centres as it avoids radiation and provides comprehensive anatomical assessment.
recommendations.special_notes: Combined anatomical and functional information. Requires sedation in young children.

recommendations.rationale: First-line cross-sectional imaging. Evaluates entire urothelium, detects bladder mass, local invasion, lymph nodes, and upper tract involvement. Essential for staging.
recommendations.india_context: Bladder cancer is the second most common urological malignancy in India. Squamous cell carcinoma of bladder associated with Schistosomiasis and chronic catheterisation more common in India than the West.
recommendations.special_notes: Muscle invasion (T2+) seen as loss of fat plane with bladder wall. Lymph node assessment critical. Combined with cystoscopy for complete evaluation.

recommendations.rationale: First-line for haematuria at primary level. Can detect bladder masses >5 mm. Assess wall thickness and vascularity with Doppler. Does not stage or detect upper tract lesions.
recommendations.india_context: Widely available. Useful initial investigation before proceeding to CTU.
recommendations.special_notes: Must be performed with full bladder. Intraluminal masses with vascularity on Doppler are suspicious. Small flat tumours (CIS) may be missed.

recommendations.rationale: Superior for local T-staging of bladder cancer (VI-RADS classification). Distinguishes non-muscle invasive (T1) from muscle-invasive (T2+) bladder cancer — critical for treatment decision.
recommendations.india_context: VI-RADS MRI is now standard for muscle-invasive assessment before radical cystectomy at oncology centres. Available at major hospitals.
recommendations.special_notes: VI-RADS 1-2 = low probability of muscle invasion; 3 = equivocal; 4-5 = high probability. DWI critical for VI-RADS.

recommendations.rationale: For nodal and distant staging in muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC). Urinary FDG excretion limits bladder assessment but useful for metastatic disease evaluation.
recommendations.india_context: Used for MIBC staging before neoadjuvant chemotherapy decisions. Available at major oncology centres.
recommendations.special_notes: FDG PET limited for primary bladder lesion (urinary excretion). Most useful for lymph node and distant metastases.
